case_name:
10.1  While  expert  evidence  may  guide  the  Committee,  the 
views  of experts do not necessarily determine the ultimate outcome
(B v Medical
Council of New Zealand) [(noted at[2005] 3 NZLR 810)]although the Court of
Appeal in F v Medical Practitioners Disciplinary Tribunal [[2005] 3 NZLR 774]
stated that the evidence of accepted
practice is “highly
relevant.”

summary.brief:
10.1  While  expert  evidence  may  guide  the  Committee,  the 
views  of experts do not necessarily determine the ultimate outcome
(B v Medical
Council of New Zealand) [(noted at[2005] 3 NZLR 810)]although the Court of
Appeal in F v Medical Practitioners Disciplinary Tribunal [[2005] 3 NZLR 774]
stated that the evidence of accepted
practice is “highly
relevant.”. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: A Charge comprising 20 Particulars of misconduct has been brought against him by a Professional Conduct Committee (PCC) of the Medical Council of New Zealand (MCNZ).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Nineteen of the 20 Particulars of the Charge referred to various drugs and Dr Twentyman’s inappropriate involvement with these; and the 20th Particular referred to retrospective making or alteration of notes concerning certain patients.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal finds that 15 of the 20 Particulars of the Charge are made out and cumulatively warrant disciplinary sanction and that many of them separately warrant such disciplinary sanction.
evidence_index.speaker: Tribunal finding

evidence_index.text: The registration of Dr Twentyman as a medical practitioner is suspended pursuant to section 100(1)(b) of the HPCA Act for a period of 9 months.
evidence_index.speaker: Tribunal order

evidence_index.text: Dr Twentyman is ordered in respect to Particular 20 to pay a fine of $7,000.00 pursuant to section 101(1)(e) of the HPCA Act.
evidence_index.speaker: Tribunal order

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal orders pursuant to section 101(1)(f) of the HPCA Act that Dr Twentyman pay the sum of $135,000.00 as a contribution to the costs of the PCC and the Tribunal, to be divided equally between them.
evidence_index.speaker: Tribunal order

evidence_index.text: The purpose of the disciplinary procedure is the protection of the public by the maintenance of professional standards.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: The Charge was heard over four days in Auckland. The parties were represented by counsel.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Particular 20 relates to the making or alteration of notes of entries retrospectively in respect of six specific patients.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The misconduct amounted, it was submitted, to such serious negligence as to portray indifference and was an abuse of the privileges which accompany registration as a medical practitioner.
evidence_index.speaker: PCC submission

requestables.name: Testosterone Level
requestables.description: Serum testosterone level to assess for hypogonadism

reveal.decision_impact: Lack of testosterone measurement impacts diagnosis and treatment decisions

reveal.clinical_significance: Monitoring for polycythaemia is important in testosterone therapy
reveal.decision_impact: Lack of monitoring could lead to undetected complications

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient P presents with symptoms suggestive of testosterone deficiency

key_events.event: Patient P presents with erectile dysfunction and low libido

timeline_phases.name: Initial Workup
timeline_phases.description: Assessment and decision-making regarding testosterone therapy

key_events.event: Blood tests ordered but testosterone not measured

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient P presented with symptoms suggestive of testosterone deficiency
decision_points.prompt: What initial investigations should be conducted before prescribing testosterone?

options.description: Order serum testosterone level and consult endocrinologist
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to confirm hypogonadism before treatment
Inadequate assessment of clinical basis for testosterone therapy

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant did not conduct baseline testosterone measurement

expected_action_court.description: Baseline testosterone level should have been measured
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence and guidelines require confirmation of hypogonadism

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to confirm hypogonadism before treatment
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Baseline testosterone level should have been measured and endocrinologist consulted

patient_outcome.description: Patient P received testosterone therapy without confirmed hypogonadism
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Potential risk of adverse effects due to inappropriate testosterone therapy

key_findings: Failure to confirm hypogonadism before prescribing testosterone
Inadequate follow-up and monitoring of testosterone therapy

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to conduct baseline testosterone measurement
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Baseline measurement would have informed appropriate treatment

factual_timeline.event: Patient P presents with erectile dysfunction and low libido
Blood tests ordered but testosterone not measured

treatments.outcome: Inappropriate due to lack of confirmed hypogonadism

allegations.allegation: Failure to confirm hypogonadism before prescribing testosterone
